abstract:
In this interdisciplinary study, Holmqvist offers an original and provocative account of the evolution of Western strategic thinking, exploring how twenty-first-century military interventions have been reimagined as exercises in global policing. By examining the US-led wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, the author demonstrates how contemporary liberal thought has reframed war as a management tool designed to restore the social contract and maintain "order" in foreign territories.

Holmqvist argues that these conflicts were characterized by a "post-adversarial" logic, where opponents were cast as criminal elements rather than political antagonists. Drawing on social theory and philosophy, the book deconstructs the intellectual scaffolding—encompassing concepts of time, space, and agency—that allowed this "fantasy" of policing to resonate so deeply among policymakers and academics alike. By tracing these developments from their pre-9/11 roots to their devastating consequences in the field, Holmqvist provides a sophisticated reappraisal of how modern liberalism rationalizes political violence. This work is an essential resource for scholars of international relations, military strategy, and the sociology of war.
